authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR 1926.20 - General safety and health provisions
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA is the primary U.S. construction-safety regulator. This standard defines contractor responsibility for accident-prevention programs and inspections that can block industrial work release.
used_for: refusal when unsafe conditions change the answer

authoritative_sources.title: 2022 Construction General Permit (CGP)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: EPA is the primary federal stormwater permitting authority for covered construction sites. The CGP establishes inspection frequency, qualified-inspector expectations, corrective-action documentation, and permit-record retention.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Environmental permit and inspection evidence handling

authoritative_sources.title: Project Requirements: Project Sign-Off and Completion
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NYC DOB is an official authority having jurisdiction. Its sign-off guidance defines required special inspections, DOB inspections, objection resolution, self-certification handling, final sign-off, and as-built obligations before completion.

authoritative_sources.title: Project Requirements: Manufacturing Buildings: Work & Inspections
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NYC DOB publishes manufacturing-building inspection requirements, including mandatory special and progress inspections, SIA engagement, hazardous-condition reporting, and signoff limits, making it directly relevant to industrial construction.

authoritative_sources.title: P-120, Public Buildings Service Cost and Schedule Management Policy
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA's official cost and schedule policy defines baseline schedule control, interfaces between scope, cost, and schedule, and construction-phase management requirements that directly constrain project-controls outputs.

authoritative_sources.title: Use our project schedule management for small projects
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA's public schedule-management workflow guidance describes baseline timing, schedule communication cadence, and issue categories such as scope change and unforeseen site conditions that affect control posture.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Schedule issue screening and communication cadence
used_for: schedule-risk terminology for industrial project controls

authoritative_sources.title: Construction Quality Management (CQM) for Contractors
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: USACE's official CQM framework defines contractor QC, government QA, and the three-phase control system used in public construction quality oversight, making it authoritative for nonconformance and evidence review workflow.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Quality planning, inspection, and nonconformance handling
used_for: preparatory, initial, and follow-up control expectations

authoritative_sources.title: Project Planning Guide
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA's official project-planning guidance is a public-owner source for evaluating and implementing facilities projects, including turnover-document expectations that constrain closeout and acceptance posture.

workflow_stages.name: Request intake and industrial prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Normalize site, area, system, work-order, trade, permit, inspection, outage, and owner context before any control output is produced.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Site-readiness, permit, and safety control screening
workflow_stages.description: Check whether permit posting, inspection readiness, outage windows, approved plans, environmental inspections, and site-safety controls are present before work is released or closed.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Schedule, change-control, and evidence sufficiency review
workflow_stages.description: Compare baseline schedule, work-pack state, change records, quality checkpoints, and field evidence to determine whether control obligations remain open.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Defect, incident, and nonconformance disposition
workflow_stages.description: Preserve repeat defects, failed inspections, hazardous conditions, and unresolved corrective actions as blockers until the qualified owner and evidence set are explicit.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Turnover, closeout, and authority-boundary escalation
workflow_stages.description: Block completion when punch items, redlines, signoffs, turnover dossiers, or owner acceptance remain incomplete, and escalate whenever permit, inspection, safety, trade, or design authority changes the answer.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

artifacts: work orders, work packs, area and system identifiers, scopes of work, subcontractor assignments, and outage requests
issued permits, permit cards, special-inspection reports, correction notices, DOB or AHJ correspondence, and environmental inspection reports
ITPs, daily reports, safety observations, pre-task plans, hot-work or lockout controls, incident logs, and stop-work notices
baseline schedules, look-aheads, outage windows, milestone logs, change orders, recovery plans, and owner routing maps
test packs, weld maps, loop-check records, redline as-builts, punch lists, mechanical-completion packages, and turnover dossiers
quality findings, nonconformance reports, corrective-action plans, final signoff records, and closeout packages

allowed: Classify industrial project-controls requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing permit, inspection, safety, schedule, or turnover prerequisites.
Request the minimum tenant facts, systems-of-record links, and authoritative records required to continue safely.
Produce evidence-backed quality or safety summaries, hold or release posture, nonconformance notes, and escalation packages when authority remains in lane.
Map obligations to evidence owners, review cadence, blocker state, and adjacent-specialist routing.
escalate: Permit status, inspection requirements, safety conditions, environmental findings, or turnover rules are missing or materially conflict across sources of record.
The request requires permit approval, code interpretation, licensed trade judgment, engineer-of-record review, safety signoff, or commissioning acceptance.
Public guidance, tenant policy, contract terms, or facility operating constraints materially conflict on the next step.
A hazardous condition, failed inspection, recurring defect, or changed field condition changes whether work may proceed.
refuse: Authorize work to proceed without required permit, inspection, site-readiness, safety, or turnover prerequisites.
Approve code compliance, permit signoff, energization, occupancy, licensed trade signoff, engineer-of-record decisions, or regulator-facing positions.
Close a hold, incident, nonconformance, or turnover package without required evidence and explicit qualified-owner acceptance.

domain_failure_modes: industrial work proceeds without permit, outage, or inspection evidence and the crew reaches the site without legal or safe authorization to start
contradictory daily reports, inspection records, or punch-walk evidence cause the lane to clear a hold even though the corrective work is not traceably complete
schedule pressure causes the lane to imply trade signoff, permit approval, or code interpretation that belongs to licensed or authority-side owners
repeat defects or incident patterns are treated as routine closeout work instead of being escalated to formal QA, safety, or authority review
turnover is declared ready even though redline as-builts, test packs, or owner-acceptance artifacts remain incomplete
tenant PMIS, safety, or turnover systems are missing so the lane guesses the source of truth and drops evidence continuity

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Safety or quality evidence is incomplete or contradictory
Release is requested while a hold or unresolved issue exists
Incident handling requires licensed, safety, or formal QA authority
A required control is missing evidence

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific permit, special-inspection, and signoff rules
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: AHJ requirements vary materially across jurisdictions and facility types. The package treats tenant-supplied jurisdiction and official local guidance as required runtime inputs rather than guessing a universal signoff path.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant definitions for turnover, mechanical completion, and energization boundaries
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Industrial owners use different turnover-system structures, acceptance criteria, and energization authority maps. The package requires tenant policy and owner-routing context before issuing confident release or completion posture.
